│ │ # Storybook Setup │ ## Project Info │ | Property | Value | │ |----------|-------| │ | Version | 10.4.0-alpha.10 | │ | Renderer | @storybook/react | │ | Framework | @storybook/react-vite | │ | Builder | @storybook/builder-vite | │ | Config Dir | `.storybook` | │ | CSF Format | CSF Factory | │ | Addons | @storybook/addon-onboarding, @storybook/addon-themes, │ @storybook/addon-docs, @storybook/addon-designs, │ @storybook/addon-vitest, @storybook/addon-a11y, │ storybook-addon-pseudo-states, @chromatic-com/storybook | │ Attention: The following instructions must be followed in order to │ successfully set up Storybook in this project. Do not skip steps or │ attempt to do them out of order. │ Your goal is to make Storybook fully functional in this project by │ analyzing the codebase, │ configuring the preview with the right decorators, and writing stories │ for some components. │ The end state should be a Storybook where any component — from a small │ button to a full page — can be added without story-specific │ workarounds. All necessary providers, CSS, browser state, and network │ mocks should live in the shared preview so that just rendering the │ component in the story is enough. │ After each created story, run Vitest to verify it renders. │ If the test fails, read the error, fix the issue, and re-run until it │ passes before moving on. │ - Copy real patterns from the codebase │ - Keep the app code unchanged │ - Put the default setup in `.storybook/preview.tsx` │ - Keep app mocking and runtime setup in `.storybook/preview.tsx`, not │ in the stories │ ### Storybook Documentation Reference │ Use the following references to look up Storybook APIs, concepts, or │ examples: │ - Full docs index: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/llms.txthttps://storybook.js.org/llms.txt]8;; │ - See code snippets only with codeOnly=true param e.g. │ ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=ts&codeOnly=truehttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=ts&codeOnly=true]8;; │ Key documentation pages for this task: │ - Writing stories: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories...]8;; │ - Decorators: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/decorators.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/deco...]8;; │ - Args: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/args.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/args.md?re...]8;; │ - Play functions: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/play-function.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/...]8;; │ - Vitest integration: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-tests/vitest-plugin.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-test...]8;; │ Fetch these URLs directly when you need guidance on Storybook APIs or │ patterns. │ ### Step 1: Analyze the codebase │ Read enough of the app to understand the full runtime environment │ before writing any stories. │ Do not stop at `main.tsx` or `App.tsx`. │ Follow imports into providers, pages, hooks, and shared components │ until you know: │ - which providers exist │ - which CSS files are injected │ - which queries fetch data │ - which browser-state reads happen │ - which portals and portal roots exist │ - which pages and components show the real usage patterns │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // src/main.tsx │ import "./index.css"; │ import App from "./App"; │ import { SessionProvider } from "./contexts/SessionContext"; │ createRoot(document.getElementById("root")!).render( │ │ │ , │ ); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should copy: │ - the `index.css` import │ - the `SessionProvider` │ - the same provider order │ Example of tracing the app deeper: │ ```tsx │ // src/App.tsx │ function App() { │ const { products, loadMoreProducts } = useProducts(); │ const { currentUser, signOut } = useSession(); │ // ... │ } │ ``` │ ```ts │ // src/hooks/useProducts.ts │ const response = await fetch(apiBaseUrl + "/products?page=1"); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // src/hooks/useTheme.ts │ const savedTheme = localStorage.getItem("theme"); │ ``` │ That means the default Storybook setup should discover and prepare: │ - provider state │ - MSW handlers for queries │ - browser-state values that are actually read during render │ ### Step 2: Build one default app environment in preview │ Set up Storybook once so most stories work without story-specific │ setup. │ Start with the smallest faithful environment: │ - the real provider tree │ - the real root CSS │ - seeded browser state if the app reads it during render │ - MSW for network/data queries │ It is fine to seed browser state such as `localStorage`, │ `sessionStorage`, and cookies when the app reads them during render. │ Seed only the specific app-owned keys and values you need. │ Do not clear all `localStorage`, `sessionStorage`, or cookies, and do │ not reset Storybook's own state. │ Do not mock or redefine the browser runtime itself. │ The stories run in Vitest browser mode, so the real browser │ environment should already exist. │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; │ import { SessionProvider } from '../src/contexts/SessionContext'; │ export default definePreview({ │ decorators: [ │ (Story) => ( │ │ │ │ ), │ ], │ async beforeEach() { │ localStorage.setItem('theme', 'dark'); │ localStorage.setItem('sidebar:open', 'true'); │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ Use this same idea for: │ - providers │ - root CSS │ - browser state │ - dates, and if the app logic depends on them during render then │ always use `mockdate` │ Example with the `mockdate` package: │ ```tsx │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; │ export default definePreview({ │ async beforeEach() { │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ ### Step 3: Support portals with preview-body.html │ If the app uses portals, copy that setup into Storybook too. │ Look for patterns like: │ - `createPortal(...)` │ - modal, dialog, drawer, popover, tooltip, toast, or dropdown portal │ components │ - hard-coded roots such as `#portal-root`, `#modal-root`, │ `#drawer-root`, or `#toast-root` │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // real component │ return createPortal(, │ document.getElementById("portal-root")!); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should create the same portal root in │ `.storybook/preview-body.html`: │ ```html │ │
│ ``` │ If the app uses multiple portal roots, create all of them there: │ ```html │ │
│
│
│ ``` │ If a library portals directly to `document.body`, do not add extra │ roots for it. │ Make sure the copied page shell, CSS, and layout still allow overlays, │ fixed positioning, and z-index stacking to render correctly. │ ### Step 4: Mock side effects globally │ All network/data queries should be handled by the default Storybook │ environment. │ - Always use `msw-storybook-addon` for query mocking. │ - If you introduce MSW, run `npx msw init ./public --save` to create │ the worker file. │ - Make sure Storybook serves `./public` as a static dir so │ `mockServiceWorker.js` is available. │ - Do not mock `fetch` directly. │ - Network/data queries should return deterministic mock data. │ - If you need to change dependencies, first check the lockfile and use │ that package manager for the change. │ Example of copying a real fetch pattern into shared handlers: │ ```ts │ // real app hook │ const response = await fetch( │ apiBaseUrl + │ "/products?" + │ new URLSearchParams({ │ page: "1", │ sort: "featured", │ }), │ ); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/msw-handlers.ts │ import { http, HttpResponse } from "msw"; │ export const mswHandlers = { │ products: [ │ http.get("]8;;https://api.example.com/products",https://api.example.com/products",]8;; │ () => │ HttpResponse.json({ │ items: [ │ { │ id: "product-1", │ name: "Example product", │ description: "Mock product description", │ imageUrl: │ "]8;;https://images.example.com/product.jpg",https://images.example.com/product.jpg",]8;; │ price: 42, │ }, │ ], │ }), │ ), │ ], │ }; │ ``` │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; │ import { initialize, mswLoader } from 'msw-storybook-addon'; │ import { mswHandlers } from './msw-handlers'; │ initialize({ │ onUnhandledRequest: 'bypass', │ }); │ export default definePreview({ │ loaders: [mswLoader], │ parameters: { │ msw: { │ handlers: mswHandlers, │ }, │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/main.ts │ import type { StorybookConfig } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ const config: StorybookConfig = { │ staticDirs: ["../public"], │ }; │ export default config; │ ``` │ Keep these mocks global. │ Do not put fetch mocks in individual stories. │ Only add handlers for requests that the shared preview setup or the │ stories actually use. │ Do not add catch-all handlers that can hide unrelated failures. │ If the defaults are not enough, improve the shared default setup │ instead. │ Seed browser state when needed, but do not mock `window`, `document`, │ `navigator`, observers, or similar runtime APIs. │ The only exception is `mockdate` when date-based rendering exists. │ ### Step 5: Write stories │ Try to find around 10 good candidate components for story files. │ Write colocated stories for top-level components, from low-level │ reusable components up to page components. │ Write up to 10 story files, or fewer only if the codebase clearly has │ fewer meaningful targets. │ The stories should use JSX copied from real usage patterns in: │ - pages │ - app shells │ - routes │ - tests │ - existing feature code │ As a rule of thumb, each story file should have around 3 story exports │ when the component or page has enough meaningful states. │ It can have more when the real usage supports it, up to 10 story │ exports in one file. │ Always show all imports explicitly in story and preview files. │ Do not rely on omitted or implied imports in examples or generated │ code. │ #### Story tags │ Every story meta must include the `ai-generated` tag to identify │ AI-created stories: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { SomeComponent } from './SomeComponent'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const Default = meta.story({ │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ If a story could not be fully fixed after the self-healing loop (the │ test still fails │ or the rendering is incomplete), add the `needs-work` tag alongside │ `ai-generated`: │ ```ts │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated', 'needs-work'], │ }); │ ``` │ #### Args vs render │ For simple components where props drive the state, prefer `args` │ stories — no `render` function needed: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { Button } from './Button'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: Button, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const Primary = meta.story({ │ args: { │ variant: 'primary', │ children: 'Save', │ }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i │ })).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }); │ export const Disabled = meta.story({ │ args: { │ variant: 'primary', │ disabled: true, │ children: 'Save', │ }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeDisabled(); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ Use `render` when the story needs composition — wrapping the component │ in layout, combining multiple components, or passing children as JSX: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { Button } from './Button'; │ import { Card } from './Card'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: Button, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const InsideCard = meta.story({ │ render: () => ( │ │ │ │ ), │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i │ })).toBeVisible(); │ await userEvent.click(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i })); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ Keep app mocking and runtime setup in preview, not in the stories. │ Do not build large story-specific harnesses. │ Do not write story files for subcomponents, hooks, contexts, or │ helpers. │ Do not create new application components. │ Do not add a custom `title`. │ Do not stop after only a few easy targets if the codebase has more │ meaningful components or pages available. │ ### Step 6: Write a play function for every story │ Every named story export must have a `play` function. │ The `play` function is not optional, even for simple stories. │ The purpose of the `play` function is to prove that the story actually │ works in the copied Storybook environment: │ - the story renders something real and non-empty │ - the decorators provide the needed context │ - the CSS is applied well enough for the intended state to be visible │ - the MSW mocks or seeded browser state are actually being used │ - important interactions, async loading states, and portals behave │ correctly │ Use `play` functions to verify behavior, not just to click around. │ A story without assertions is incomplete. │ Use tools from `storybook/test` such as: │ - `expect` │ - `waitFor` │ Prefer `canvas` and `userEvent` from the `play` context. │ Do not destructure `canvasElement` just to create `const canvas = │ within(canvasElement)`. │ Do not import `userEvent` from `storybook/test`; use `userEvent` from │ the `play` context instead. │ Only use `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when you need to query outside │ the canvas, such as for portals. │ Example: │ ```tsx │ import type { StoryObj } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ export const FilledForm: Story = { │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ const emailInput = canvas.getByLabelText("email", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(emailInput, "example-email@email.com", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const passwordInput = canvas.getByLabelText("password", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(passwordInput, "ExamplePassword", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const submitButton = canvas.getByRole("button"); │ await userEvent.click(submitButton); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ The assertions should match the real pattern you copied: │ - for provider-backed stories, assert the provider-dependent UI │ appears correctly │ - for mocked-data stories, wait for the mocked data to appear and │ assert on it │ - for CSS-sensitive states, assert on visibility, text layout, │ class-driven states, or meaningful computed styles │ - for routing or navigation stories, assert the routed state or │ navigation outcome │ - for portal stories, query from `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when │ the UI renders outside the canvas │ Examples of useful checks: │ - a themed button has the expected label and is visibly enabled or │ disabled │ - a modal opened through a decorator or provider is visible in the │ portal root │ - mocked API data appears in the page instead of a loading spinner │ forever │ - a selected tab actually shows the selected panel │ - a toast, alert, or badge has the expected accessible text and visual │ state │ - a CSS class or computed style confirms the real state that matters │ ### Step 7: Prove CSS is loaded in exactly one story named `CssCheck` │ In exactly one story, named `CssCheck`, assert a component-specific │ computed style. `toBeVisible` passes on an unstyled component; a │ concrete style value proves the shared preview loaded the app's CSS. │ Pick a visually distinctive component, read a styling value from its │ source, and assert it with `getComputedStyle`: │ ```tsx │ export const CssCheck: Story = { │ args: { children: "Submit" }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ const button = canvas.getByRole("button", { name: /submit/i }); │ // PrimaryButton uses bg-blue-600 — fails if Tailwind / global CSS did │ not load. │ await expect(getComputedStyle(button).backgroundColor).toBe("rgb(37, │ 99, 235)"); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ ### Step 8: Cover the patterns you found │ Write stories for the real patterns in the codebase, for example: │ - a low-level reusable component in real JSX usage │ - a provider-backed component │ - a browser-state-backed component │ - a fetched-data component │ - a real page component │ Use `App.tsx` to inspect the real provider tree and usage patterns, │ but do not make a story for `App` when the codebase has actual page │ components. │ Example page story: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { ProductPage } from './ProductPage'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: ProductPage, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const Default = meta.story({ │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect( │ canvas.getByRole('heading', { name: /products/i }), │ ).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ ### Step 9: Verify both rendering and types │ As you work, verify the stories with Vitest: │ ```bash │ npx vitest --project storybook │ ``` │ Also verify types so you catch missing required props, broken imports, │ and preview typing issues. Run the same TypeScript command the project │ itself uses. │ ```bash │ │ ``` │ After verification passes, review every changed file and remove │ anything that is not needed for the final solution, especially debug │ fixes, overly broad mocks, unnecessary dependencies, and eval │ artifacts. │ Keep iterating until: │ - every story you wrote passes │ - every story you wrote has a meaningful passing `play` function │ - the changed stories and preview setup pass the project's real │ TypeScript check │ - the rendered output looks sensible │ - the default global mocked environment is strong enough that stories │ do not need manual fetch overrides │ - stories no longer fail because the shared preview setup and story │ JSX are fixed │ - all passing stories have `tags: ['ai-generated']` in their meta │ - any stories that still need work have `tags: ['ai-generated', │ 'needs-work']` in their meta

Thoroughly explore this project to understand:
1. Project structure - list all directories and key files
2. What's in src/main.tsx and src/App.tsx - follow all imports to understand the full provider tree
3. All context providers used
4. All CSS files imported at the root level
5. All hooks that fetch data (look for fetch, axios, useSWR, useQuery, etc.)
6. All browser state reads (localStorage, sessionStorage, cookies)
7. All portal usage (createPortal, modal roots, etc.)
8. All pages and route definitions
9. All reusable components
10. Package manager used (check for lock files)
11. Existing .storybook configuration
12. TypeScript configuration and type-check command
13. Any existing test setup

Be very thorough - read every provider, hook, and page component file completely.

{
  "name": "edgy",
  "version": "2.0.0",
  "private": true,
  "type": "module",
  "scripts": {
    "dev": "vite",
    "build": "vite build",
    "preview": "vite preview",
    "server": "node api/index.mjs",
    "check-deps": "npm-check",
    "storybook": "storybook dev -p 6006",
    "build-storybook": "storybook build",
    "chromatic": "chromatic --exit-zero-on-changes --only-changed",
    "vitest:storybook": "vitest run --project=storybook"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "@headlessui/react": "^1.5.0",
    "@tailwindcss/vite": "^4.1.11",
    "airtable": "^0.12.2",
    "cors": "^2.8.5",
    "dotenv": "^17.2.0",
    "express": "^4.18.2",
    "moment": "^2.30.1",
    "node-fetch": "^3.3.2",
    "react": "^18.3.1",
    "react-dom": "^18.3.1",
    "react-google-recaptcha": "^3.1.0",
    "react-router-dom": "^7.7.0",
    "uuid": "^11.1.0"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "@types/uuid": "^10.0.0",
    "@vitejs/plugin-react": "^4.6.0",
    "npm-check": "^6.0.1",
    "vite": "^7.0.5",
    "storybook": "0.0.0-pr-34595-sha-01068aa6",
    "@storybook/react-vite": "0.0.0-pr-34595-sha-01068aa6",
    "@chromatic-com/storybook": "^5.1.1",
    "@storybook/addon-vitest": "0.0.0-pr-34595-sha-01068aa6",
    "@storybook/addon-a11y": "0.0.0-pr-34595-sha-01068aa6",
    "@storybook/addon-docs": "0.0.0-pr-34595-sha-01068aa6",
    "@storybook/addon-onboarding": "0.0.0-pr-34595-sha-01068aa6",
    "prop-types": "^15.8.1",
    "vitest": "^4.1.2",
    "playwright": "^1.58.2",
    "@vitest/browser-playwright": "^4.1.2",
    "@vitest/coverage-v8": "^4.1.2"
  }
}

![Edgy](https://github.com/catherineisonline/edgy/blob/main/public/project-preview.png?raw=true) # Edgy - #1 Instagram Growth Agency [![GitHub license](https://img.shields.io/github/license/catherineisonline/edgy)](https://github.com/catherineisonline/edgy/blob/main/LICENSE) [![GitHub issues](https://img.shields.io/github/issues/catherineisonline/edgy)](https://github.com/catherineisonline/edgy/issues) [![GitHub stars](https://img.shields.io/github/stars/catherineisonline/edgy)](https://github.com/catherineisonline/edgy/stargazers) ## Introduction Welcome to the Edgy Social Media Marketing Agency's full-stack website! At Edgy, we're not just a business; we're your partners in navigating the dynamic landscape of Instagram marketing. Whether you're a budding influencer or a business looking to boost your brand's online visibility, we're here to help you stand out in the digital crowd. #### About Edgy: Edgy is a fictional social media marketing agency that specializes in Instagram growth strategies. Our team of passionate experts understands the ever-changing algorithms, trends, and techniques that define success on Instagram. With our tailored services and creative solutions, we're dedicated to helping you harness the power of this platform to its fullest potential. #### What We Offer: Comprehensive Services: Our website offers a diverse range of services designed to cater to both businesses and beginner influencers. From content creation and engagement strategies to influencer collaborations and brand partnerships, we've got you covered. Flexible Pricing Options: We understand that every journey is unique, which is why we offer a variety of pricing plans that can be customized to suit your specific needs and budget. Whether you're just starting out or looking to scale, we have a plan for you. Engaging Blog Content: Stay up-to-date with the latest trends, tips, and insights in the world of Instagram marketing through our informative blog. Our team of experts shares valuable information to empower you on your digital journey. Career Opportunities: Passionate about social media marketing? Explore exciting career prospects with Edgy on our dedicated career page. Join our team of creatives and strategists to make a lasting impact in the digital marketing space. #### Get in Touch: Contact Form: Have questions, inquiries, or simply want to say hello? Our contact form is designed to make reaching out to us quick and easy. We value your input and are always ready to assist you. ## Disclaimer Please note that Edgy is a fictional business created for illustrative purposes. While our website and services are designed to provide insights into the world of social media marketing, we do not provide real services or consultations. This platform is intended to showcase a hypothetical business model. Thank you for visiting Edgy's website. We look forward to being a part of your journey towards Instagram success! Disclaimer: This is a fictional business created for demonstration purposes. ## Features The website offers a seamless user experience through its comprehensive account management features. Users have the ability to perform essential actions such as signing up, logging in, and modifying their profile information. #### Sign Up and Login: - Users can easily create an account by providing their email address and password during the registration process. - Upon successful registration, users are granted access to their personalized accounts, enabling them to log in securely. #### Profile Information: - The user's profile is designed to be minimalistic, containing essential details to maintain simplicity and user-friendliness.

content.text:
Perfect! Now let me compile a comprehensive report of my findings: ## THOROUGH PROJECT EXPLORATION REPORT ### 1. PROJECT STRUCTURE & OVERVIEW This is **Edgy**, a full-stack fictional Instagram Growth Agency website built with React, TypeScript configuration, and Storybook. It uses: - **Package Manager**: npm (package-lock.json present) - **Build Tool**: Vite - **React Version**: 18.3.1 - **CSS Framework**: Tailwind CSS v4 with @tailwindcss/vite - **Styling**: Core-UI with tailwindcss imports at root **Root Directory Structure**: ``` /api - Backend Express server /public - Static assets /src - Main application source ├── airtable/ - Airtable integration ├── assets/ - Images and SVGs ├── components/ - Reusable components ├── core-ui/ - Global CSS (index.css) ├── data/ - Static data files ├── routes/ - Page routes ├── stories/ - Storybook stories ├── App.jsx - Main app with routing └── index.jsx - Entry point /.storybook - Storybook configuration ``` --- ### 2. ENTRY POINTS & PROVIDER TREE **src/index.jsx** (Entry Point): ```javascript - Creates React root - Imports core-ui/index.css (Tailwind) - Renders App component ``` **src/App.jsx** (Full Provider Tree - NO CONTEXT PROVIDERS): ``` (react-router-dom v7.7.0) ├── - Header with auth-aware nav ├── │ ├── / → │ ├── /contact → │ ├── /pricing → │ ├── /faq → │ ├── /blog → │ ├── /blog/:name → │ ├── /about → │ ├── /terms → │ ├── /refunds → │ ├── /privacy → │ ├── /jobs → │ ├── /sign-in → or (if logged in) │ ├── /sign-up → or (if logged in) │ ├── /profile → (if logged in) or │ └── * → ├──
``` **No Context Providers** - State is managed at App level and passed via props. --- ### 3. GLOBAL CSS IMPORTS **src/core-ui/index.css** (Imported at root): ```css @import "tailwindcss"; :root { --primary-font-color: rgb(147, 157, 250); --primary-font-color-hover: rgb(82, 98, 245); } /* Body reset, font families, code styling */ ``` **Component-level CSS files** (not imported at root): - src/stories/button.css - src/stories/header.css - src/stories/page.css --- ### 4. DATA FETCHING HOOKS & METHODS **Fetching Patterns Used** (NO useSWR, useQuery, or custom hooks - direct fetch): 1. **App.jsx - useEffect hooks**: - Checks sessionStorage on mount for user persistence - Two useEffect calls: one for initialization, one for logout handling - `retrieveDatabase(email, password?)` - fetch from VITE_AIRTABLE_SERVER_URL - `updateUser(userId, formValue)` - POST to update user fields - `registerUser(data)` - Uses Airtable SDK directly 2. **Form Components** (SignIn, SignUp, Contact, Newsletter, Profile): - Direct `fetch()` calls in event handlers - No async/await in some cases, Promise-based in others - Captcha verification via fetch to VITE_SERVER_URL - Airtable operations via imported `edgyBase` instance 3. **BlogPost.jsx**: - `useEffect` to fetch blog post data from static blogPostsData --- ### 5. BROWSER STATE READS **sessionStorage** (from App.jsx): - `sessionStorage.getItem("loggedIn")` - Checks if user is logged in - `sessionStorage.setItem("loggedIn", "true")` - Sets on login - `sessionStorage.removeItem("loggedIn")` - Clears on logout - `sessionStorage.getItem("user")` - Retrieves user object (JSON string) - `sessionStorage.setItem("user", JSON.stringify(userData))` - Stores user object - `sessionStorage.removeItem("user")` - Clears on logout **No localStorage or cookies used**. --- ### 6. PORTAL USAGE **Modal Dialogs** (Not using React Portals - just conditional rendering with fixed positioning): In **Profile.jsx**: - Delete account confirmation modal: ```jsx {deleteRequest && (
{/* Modal content */}
)} ``` - Change password modal: ```jsx {editPassword && (
{/* Form content */}
)} ``` **No createPortal() used** - Just CSS fixed positioning with z-index management. --- ### 7. PAGES & ROUTE DEFINITIONS All routes defined in App.jsx with 12 main routes: | Path | Component | Notes | |------|-----------|-------| | / | Landing | Home page, loggedIn prop | | /contact | Contact | Contact form page | | /pricing | Pricing | Pricing page, loggedIn prop | | /faq | Faq | FAQ page | | /blog | Blog | Blog listing | | /blog/:name | BlogPost | Dynamic blog post | | /about | About | About page | | /terms | Terms | Terms of service | | /refunds | Refunds | Refund policy | | /privacy | Privacy | Privacy policy | | /jobs | Jobs | Jobs listing | | /sign-in | SignIn/Profile | Conditional (auth-aware) | | /sign-up | SignUp/Profile | Conditional (auth-aware) | | /profile | Profile | Protected (auth-required) | | * | NotFound | 404 page | --- ### 8. REUSABLE COMPONENTS **Layout Components**: - Navigation.jsx - Header with conditional auth buttons - Footer.jsx - Footer with links and social icons - NotFound.jsx - 404 page with helpful links **Form Components**: - SignInForm.jsx - Email/password form with reCAPTCHA - SignUpForm.jsx - Registration form with reCAPTCHA - ContactForm.jsx - Contact form with reCAPTCHA - Newsletter.jsx - Email subscription - JobsForm.jsx - Job application form **Page Section Components**: - Cta.jsx - Call-to-action section - Team.jsx - Team members grid (uses data/team.js) - Hero.jsx - Landing hero section - Partners.jsx - Partners section - Feature.jsx - Features section - Stats.jsx - Statistics section - Pricing.jsx - Pricing cards - Testimonials.jsx - Testimonials carousel - Comparison.jsx - Comparison table - FaqList.jsx - FAQ accordion - BlogGrid.jsx - Blog posts grid - JobsListing.jsx - Jobs list - PricingFaq.jsx - Pricing page FAQ - PricingComparisonTable.jsx - Pricing comparison **Storybook Components**: - Button.jsx - Reusable button - Header.jsx - Header story component - Page.jsx - Page story component --- ### 9. PACKAGE MANAGER **npm** with package-lock.json v3 **Key Dependencies**: ```json { "react": "^18.3.1", "react-dom": "^18.3.1", "react-router-dom": "^7.7.0", "airtable": "^0.12.2", "react-google-recaptcha": "^3.1.0", "@headlessui/react": "^1.5.0", "@tailwindcss/vite": "^4.1.11", "moment": "^2.30.1", "express": "^4.18.2", "cors": "^2.8.5", "dotenv": "^17.2.0", "uuid": "^11.1.0" } ``` **Dev Dependencies**: ```json { "vite": "^7.0.5", "@vitejs/plugin-react": "^4.6.0", "storybook": "0.0.0-pr-34595-sha-01068aa6", // PR version! "@storybook/react-vite": "0.0.0-pr-34595-sha-01068aa6", "@storybook/addon-vitest": "0.0.0-pr-34595-sha-01068aa6", "@storybook/addon-a11y": "0.0.0-pr-34595-sha-01068aa6", "@storybook/addon-docs": "0.0.0-pr-34595-sha-01068aa6", "@chromatic-com/storybook": "^5.1.1", "vitest": "^4.1.2", "playwright": "^1.58.2" } ``` --- ### 10. STORYBOOK CONFIGURATION **.storybook/main.ts**: ```typescript { stories: [ '../src/**/*.mdx', '../src/**/*.stories.@(js|jsx|mjs|ts|tsx)', './eval-support/*.mdx', ], addons: [ '@chromatic-com/storybook', '@storybook/addon-vitest', '@storybook/addon-a11y', '@storybook/addon-docs', '@storybook/addon-onboarding', ], framework: '@storybook/react-vite', } ``` **.storybook/preview.tsx**: ```typescript { parameters: { controls: { matchers: { color: /(background|color)$/i, date: /Date$/i, }, }, a11y: { test: 'todo', }, }, } ``` **Story Files**: - src/stories/Button.stories.js (5 stories) - src/stories/Header.stories.js (2 stories) - src/stories/Page.stories.js (2 stories with interaction tests) **Scripts**: ```json { "storybook": "storybook dev -p 6006", "build-storybook": "storybook build", "chromatic": "chromatic --exit-zero-on-changes --only-changed", "vitest:storybook": "vitest run --project=storybook" } ``` --- ### 11. TYPESCRIPT CONFIGURATION **No tsconfig.json found** - Project uses JSX/JavaScript files with: - .jsx files (React components) - .js files (data and utilities) - JSDoc comments where types are helpful **Vite includes**: `/// ` --- ### 12. TEST SETUP **Vitest Configuration** (vite.config.js): ```javascript test: { projects: [ { name: "storybook", browser: { enabled: true, headless: true, provider: playwright(playwrightProviderOptions), instances: [{ browser: "chromium" }], }, plugins: [storybookTest({ configDir: ".storybook" })], }, ], } ``` **Test Command**: `vitest:storybook` - runs Storybook tests with Playwright **Playwright Setup**: - Browser: Chromium - Screenshots config: Mobile device scaling (393x852, 2x scale factor) - Headless mode: true **Story Tests**: - Page.jsx has interaction tests using `play()` function - Tests use `expect`, `userEvent`, `within` from 'storybook/test' --- ### 13. DATA FILES **Static data** in src/data/: - team.js - Team member information - features.js - Feature list - blogPostsData.js - Blog post content - pricingFaq.js - Pricing FAQs - partners.js - Partner logos - companyInformation.js - Company info - faq.js - FAQ items - pricingTiers.js - Pricing plans - jobs.js - Job listings - companyStats.js - Statistics - pricingSections.js - Pricing sections --- ### 14. BACKEND SETUP **api/index.mjs** - Express server: - GET /airtable - Fetches all users - POST /airtable - Updates user fields - POST /verify-recaptcha - Validates reCAPTCHA tokens - Uses environment variables: AIRTABLE_KEY, AIRTABLE_ID, CAPTCHA_SECRET - CORS enabled for all origins --- ### 15. ENVIRONMENT VARIABLES USED In frontend code (via import.meta.env): - VITE_AIRTABLE_KEY - VITE_AIRTABLE_ID - VITE_AIRTABLE_SERVER_URL - VITE_CAPTCHA_KEY - VITE_CAPTCHA_SECRET - VITE_SERVER_URL In backend (.env.backend): - AIRTABLE_KEY - AIRTABLE_ID - CAPTCHA_SECRET - PORT (defaults to 3000) --- ### 16. STATE MANAGEMENT **No Global State Management Libraries** (no Redux, Zustand, Context API): - All state managed at App level in App.jsx - State passed via props down component tree - Uses React hooks: useState, useEffect, useRef, useNavigate **App-level state**: - user (object or null) - loggedIn (boolean) - triggeredLogout (boolean) --- ### KEY FINDINGS SUMMARY 1. **Lightweight Architecture** - No context providers, no global state management 2. **Direct Data Fetching** - Uses raw fetch() API, Airtable SDK, no data layer abstractions 3. **Browser State Only** - sessionStorage for logged-in user persistence 4. **Modal Management** - Fixed positioning CSS, not React Portals 5. **Tailwind CSS** - All styling via utility classes 6. **React Router v7** - Newest router version with modern patterns 7. **Storybook Eval** - Using pre-release/PR version for evaluation purposes 8. **Vitest + Playwright** - Test setup ready with browser-based testing 9. **Minimal Dependencies** - Only essential packages, no heavy tooling 10. **Full-Stack Ready** - Backend Express server for API/reCAPTCHA verification

11. Existing .storybook configuration
12. TypeScript configuration and type-check command
13. Any existing test setup

Be very thorough - read every provider, hook, and page component file completely.

PROJECT STRUCTURE & OVERVIEW This is **Edgy**, a full-stack fictional Instagram Growth Agency website built with React, TypeScript configuration, and Storybook. It uses: - **Package Manager**: npm (package-lock.json present) - **Build Tool**: Vite - **React Version**: 18.3.1 - **CSS Framework**: Tailwind CSS v4 with @tailwindcss/vite - **Styling**: Core-UI with tailwindcss imports at root **Root Directory Structure**: ``` /api - Backend Express server /public - Static assets /src - Main application source ├── airtable/ - Airtable integration ├── assets/ - Images and SVGs ├── components/ - Reusable components ├── core-ui/ - Global CSS (index.css) ├── data/ - Static data files ├── routes/ - Page routes ├── stories/ - Storybook stories ├── App.jsx - Main app with routing └── index.jsx - Entry point /.storybook - Storybook configuration ``` --- ### 2. ENTRY POINTS & PROVIDER TREE **src/index.jsx** (Entry Point): ```javascript - Creates React root - Imports core-ui/index.css (Tailwind) - Renders App component ``` **src/App.jsx** (Full Provider Tree - NO CONTEXT PROVIDERS): ``` (react-router-dom v7.7.0) ├── - Header with auth-aware nav ├── │ ├── / → │ ├── /contact → │ ├── /pricing → │ ├── /faq → │ ├── /blog → │ ├── /blog/:name → │ ├── /about → │ ├── /terms → │ ├── /refunds → │ ├── /privacy → │ ├── /jobs → │ ├── /sign-in → or (if logged in) │ ├── /sign-up → or (if logged in) │ ├── /profile → (if logged in) or │ └── * → ├──
``` **No Context Providers** - State is managed at App level and passed via props. --- ### 3. GLOBAL CSS IMPORTS **src/core-ui/index.css** (Imported at root): ```css @import "tailwindcss"; :root { --primary-font-color: rgb(147, 157, 250); --primary-font-color-hover: rgb(82, 98, 245); } /* Body reset, font families, code styling */ ``` **Component-level CSS files** (not imported at root): - src/stories/button.css - src/stories/header.css - src/stories/page.css --- ### 4. DATA FETCHING HOOKS & METHODS **Fetching Patterns Used** (NO useSWR, useQuery, or custom hooks - direct fetch): 1. **App.jsx - useEffect hooks**: - Checks sessionStorage on mount for user persistence - Two useEffect calls: one for initialization, one for logout handling - `retrieveDatabase(email, password?)` - fetch from VITE_AIRTABLE_SERVER_URL - `updateUser(userId, formValue)` - POST to update user fields - `registerUser(data)` - Uses Airtable SDK directly 2. **Form Components** (SignIn, SignUp, Contact, Newsletter, Profile): - Direct `fetch()` calls in event handlers - No async/await in some cases, Promise-based in others - Captcha verification via fetch to VITE_SERVER_URL - Airtable operations via imported `edgyBase` instance 3. **BlogPost.jsx**: - `useEffect` to fetch blog post data from static blogPostsData --- ### 5. BROWSER STATE READS **sessionStorage** (from App.jsx): - `sessionStorage.getItem("loggedIn")` - Checks if user is logged in - `sessionStorage.setItem("loggedIn", "true")` - Sets on login - `sessionStorage.removeItem("loggedIn")` - Clears on logout - `sessionStorage.getItem("user")` - Retrieves user object (JSON string) - `sessionStorage.setItem("user", JSON.stringify(userData))` - Stores user object - `sessionStorage.removeItem("user")` - Clears on logout **No localStorage or cookies used**. --- ### 6. PORTAL USAGE **Modal Dialogs** (Not using React Portals - just conditional rendering with fixed positioning): In **Profile.jsx**: - Delete account confirmation modal: ```jsx {deleteRequest && (
{/* Modal content */}
)} ``` - Change password modal: ```jsx {editPassword && (
{/* Form content */}
)} ``` **No createPortal() used** - Just CSS fixed positioning with z-index management. --- ### 7. PAGES & ROUTE DEFINITIONS All routes defined in App.jsx with 12 main routes: | Path | Component | Notes | |------|-----------|-------| | / | Landing | Home page, loggedIn prop | | /contact | Contact | Contact form page | | /pricing | Pricing | Pricing page, loggedIn prop | | /faq | Faq | FAQ page | | /blog | Blog | Blog listing | | /blog/:name | BlogPost | Dynamic blog post | | /about | About | About page | | /terms | Terms | Terms of service | | /refunds | Refunds | Refund policy | | /privacy | Privacy | Privacy policy | | /jobs | Jobs | Jobs listing | | /sign-in | SignIn/Profile | Conditional (auth-aware) | | /sign-up | SignUp/Profile | Conditional (auth-aware) | | /profile | Profile | Protected (auth-required) | | * | NotFound | 404 page | --- ### 8. REUSABLE COMPONENTS **Layout Components**: - Navigation.jsx - Header with conditional auth buttons - Footer.jsx - Footer with links and social icons - NotFound.jsx - 404 page with helpful links **Form Components**: - SignInForm.jsx - Email/password form with reCAPTCHA - SignUpForm.jsx - Registration form with reCAPTCHA - ContactForm.jsx - Contact form with reCAPTCHA - Newsletter.jsx - Email subscription - JobsForm.jsx - Job application form **Page Section Components**: - Cta.jsx - Call-to-action section - Team.jsx - Team members grid (uses data/team.js) - Hero.jsx - Landing hero section - Partners.jsx - Partners section - Feature.jsx - Features section - Stats.jsx - Statistics section - Pricing.jsx - Pricing cards - Testimonials.jsx - Testimonials carousel - Comparison.jsx - Comparison table - FaqList.jsx - FAQ accordion - BlogGrid.jsx - Blog posts grid - JobsListing.jsx - Jobs list - PricingFaq.jsx - Pricing page FAQ - PricingComparisonTable.jsx - Pricing comparison **Storybook Components**: - Button.jsx - Reusable button - Header.jsx - Header story component - Page.jsx - Page story component --- ### 9. PACKAGE MANAGER **npm** with package-lock.json v3 **Key Dependencies**: ```json { "react": "^18.3.1", "react-dom": "^18.3.1", "react-router-dom": "^7.7.0", "airtable": "^0.12.2", "react-google-recaptcha": "^3.1.0", "@headlessui/react": "^1.5.0", "@tailwindcss/vite": "^4.1.11", "moment": "^2.30.1", "express": "^4.18.2", "cors": "^2.8.5", "dotenv": "^17.2.0", "uuid": "^11.1.0" } ``` **Dev Dependencies**: ```json { "vite": "^7.0.5", "@vitejs/plugin-react": "^4.6.0", "storybook": "0.0.0-pr-34595-sha-01068aa6", // PR version! "@storybook/react-vite": "0.0.0-pr-34595-sha-01068aa6", "@storybook/addon-vitest": "0.0.0-pr-34595-sha-01068aa6", "@storybook/addon-a11y": "0.0.0-pr-34595-sha-01068aa6", "@storybook/addon-docs": "0.0.0-pr-34595-sha-01068aa6", "@chromatic-com/storybook": "^5.1.1", "vitest": "^4.1.2", "playwright": "^1.58.2" } ``` --- ### 10. STORYBOOK CONFIGURATION **.storybook/main.ts**: ```typescript { stories: [ '../src/**/*.mdx', '../src/**/*.stories.@(js|jsx|mjs|ts|tsx)', './eval-support/*.mdx', ], addons: [ '@chromatic-com/storybook', '@storybook/addon-vitest', '@storybook/addon-a11y', '@storybook/addon-docs', '@storybook/addon-onboarding', ], framework: '@storybook/react-vite', } ``` **.storybook/preview.tsx**: ```typescript { parameters: { controls: { matchers: { color: /(background|color)$/i, date: /Date$/i, }, }, a11y: { test: 'todo', }, }, } ``` **Story Files**: - src/stories/Button.stories.js (5 stories) - src/stories/Header.stories.js (2 stories) - src/stories/Page.stories.js (2 stories with interaction tests) **Scripts**: ```json { "storybook": "storybook dev -p 6006", "build-storybook": "storybook build", "chromatic": "chromatic --exit-zero-on-changes --only-changed", "vitest:storybook": "vitest run --project=storybook" } ``` --- ### 11. TYPESCRIPT CONFIGURATION **No tsconfig.json found** - Project uses JSX/JavaScript files with: - .jsx files (React components) - .js files (data and utilities) - JSDoc comments where types are helpful **Vite includes**: `/// ` --- ### 12. TEST SETUP **Vitest Configuration** (vite.config.js): ```javascript test: { projects: [ { name: "storybook", browser: { enabled: true, headless: true, provider: playwright(playwrightProviderOptions), instances: [{ browser: "chromium" }], }, plugins: [storybookTest({ configDir: ".storybook" })], }, ], } ``` **Test Command**: `vitest:storybook` - runs Storybook tests with Playwright **Playwright Setup**: - Browser: Chromium - Screenshots config: Mobile device scaling (393x852, 2x scale factor) - Headless mode: true **Story Tests**: - Page.jsx has interaction tests using `play()` function - Tests use `expect`, `userEvent`, `within` from 'storybook/test' --- ### 13. DATA FILES **Static data** in src/data/: - team.js - Team member information - features.js - Feature list - blogPostsData.js - Blog post content - pricingFaq.js - Pricing FAQs - partners.js - Partner logos - companyInformation.js - Company info - faq.js - FAQ items - pricingTiers.js - Pricing plans - jobs.js - Job listings - companyStats.js - Statistics - pricingSections.js - Pricing sections --- ### 14. BACKEND SETUP **api/index.mjs** - Express server: - GET /airtable - Fetches all users - POST /airtable - Updates user fields - POST /verify-recaptcha - Validates reCAPTCHA tokens - Uses environment variables: AIRTABLE_KEY, AIRTABLE_ID, CAPTCHA_SECRET - CORS enabled for all origins --- ### 15. ENVIRONMENT VARIABLES USED In frontend code (via import.meta.env): - VITE_AIRTABLE_KEY - VITE_AIRTABLE_ID - VITE_AIRTABLE_SERVER_URL - VITE_CAPTCHA_KEY - VITE_CAPTCHA_SECRET - VITE_SERVER_URL In backend (.env.backend): - AIRTABLE_KEY - AIRTABLE_ID - CAPTCHA_SECRET - PORT (defaults to 3000) --- ### 16. STATE MANAGEMENT **No Global State Management Libraries** (no Redux, Zustand, Context API): - All state managed at App level in App.jsx - State passed via props down component tree - Uses React hooks: useState, useEffect, useRef, useNavigate **App-level state**: - user (object or null) - loggedIn (boolean) - triggeredLogout (boolean) --- ### KEY FINDINGS SUMMARY 1. **Lightweight Architecture** - No context providers, no global state management 2. **Direct Data Fetching** - Uses raw fetch() API, Airtable SDK, no data layer abstractions 3. **Browser State Only** - sessionStorage for logged-in user persistence 4. **Modal Management** - Fixed positioning CSS, not React Portals 5. **Tailwind CSS** - All styling via utility classes 6. **React Router v7** - Newest router version with modern patterns 7. **Storybook Eval** - Using pre-release/PR version for evaluation purposes 8. **Vitest + Playwright** - Test setup ready with browser-based testing 9. **Minimal Dependencies** - Only essential packages, no heavy tooling 10. **Full-Stack Ready** - Backend Express server for API/reCAPTCHA verification

Whether you're passionate about fashion, travel, food, fitness, or any other niche, Instagram offers an excellent opportunity to showcase your creativity and engage with a global audience. So, if you're eager to share your passions with the world, here's a step-by-step guide on how to start an Instagram blog and make it a resounding success.\n\n1. Define Your Niche:\nBefore diving headfirst into Instagram blogging, it's crucial to identify your niche. Determine what interests you the most and where your expertise lies. Invest in a good camera or use your smartphone's advanced camera features to capture stunning images. Ensure your photos are well-lit, well-composed, and visually appealing.     33→        blogPost: `
    34→Instagram is a powerful platform for engaging with your audience, but knowing what to post can sometimes be challenging. To help you keep your feed interesting and your followers engaged, we've put together a list of content ideas that you can use to make your Instagram shine!\n\n
    35→1. Behind-the-Scenes:\n

    39→3. Inspirational Quotes:\n

    41→4. Product or Service Highlights:\n
    42→Highlight your best-selling products or most popular services. Showcase their features and benefits to entice your audience to make a purchase.\n\n
    43→5. Tutorials and How-Tos:\n
    44→Educate your followers with step-by-step tutorials and how-to guides related to your industry. Providing value through educational content can establish your brand as an authority in your niche.\n\n
    45→6. Customer Testimonials:\n
    46→Share positive feedback and testimonials from satisfied customers. Testimonials build trust and social proof, which can encourage others to do business with you.\n\n
    47→7. Polls and Surveys:\n
    48→Engage your audience by running polls and surveys in your Instagram Stories. This not only encourages interaction but also helps you gather valuable insights from your followers.\n\n
    49→8. Throwback Posts:\n
    50→Take a trip down memory lane and share old photos or milestones from your brand's history. Nostalgic content can evoke positive emotions and create a sense of nostalgia among your audience.\n\n

   234→        }, blogPost: `Running an Instagram contest can be an exciting way to engage your audience and boost brand awareness. Here's a step-by-step guide on how to run a successful Instagram contest:\n\n
   235→        1. Define Your Goals:\n
   236→        Determine the purpose of your contest. Are you looking to increase followers, generate user-generated content (UGC), or promote a new product?\n\n

   240→        Establish straightforward and easy-to-understand rules for participation, eligibility, and prize distribution.\n\n

   247→        7. Promote the Contest:\n

   340→        blogPost: `Promoting your eCommerce store on Instagram can significantly boost your online presence and drive sales. If you're looking to leverage Instagram to grow your eCommerce business, here are some effective strategies to get started:\n\n

   392→            'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1598128558393-70ff21433be0?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=MnwxMjA3fDB8MHxwaG90by1wYWdlfHx8fGVufDB8fHx8&auto=format&fit=crop&w=1089&q=80',
   393→        readingTime: '5 min',
   394→        author: {
   395→            name: 'Daniela Metz',

   398→        },
   399→        blogPost: `If you're a business looking to harness the power of Instagram for growth and success, this beginner's guide is for you. Instagram is a valuable platform for businesses to connect with their target audience, build brand awareness, and drive sales. Here's how to get started with Instagram for business:\n\n
   400→1. Set Up an Instagram Business Account:\n
   401→Create a dedicated business account on Instagram. It provides access to valuable insights and features like Instagram Ads and Shopping.\n\n
   402→2. Optimize Your Profile:\n
   403→Use your business name as your username and include a recognizable profile picture, preferably your logo. Write a compelling bio that clearly communicates your brand identity and value proposition.\n\n
   404→3. Define Your Target Audience:\n
   405→Identify your target audience and understand their preferences, interests, and pain points. This knowledge will guide your content strategy.\n\n
   406→4. Create High-Quality Visual Content:\n
   407→Invest in creating visually appealing and high-quality images and videos that represent your brand. Use professional photography and graphic design to make your content stand out.\n\n
   408→5. Develop a Content Strategy:\n
   409→Plan your content in advance to ensure consistency and relevance. Mix promotional content with valuable and entertaining posts to keep your audience engaged.\n\n
   410→6. Use Hashtags Strategically:\n
   411→Research and use relevant hashtags to reach a wider audience and increase discoverability. Create a branded hashtag to encourage user-generated content (UGC).\n\n
   412→7. Engage with Your Audience:\n
   413→Respond to comments, messages, and mentions promptly. Engage with your audience through likes, comments, and reposts of UGC.\n\n
   414→8. Utilize Instagram Stories and Highlights:\n
   415→Leverage Instagram Stories to share behind-the-scenes content, product launches, and limited-time offers. Save important Stories as Highlights on your profile to showcase them permanently.\n\n
   416→9. Collaborate with Influencers and Partners:\n
   417→Partner with influencers and complementary businesses to extend your reach and credibility.\n\n
   418→10. Run Instagram Contests and Giveaways:\n
   419→Organize contests and giveaways to boost engagement and attract new followers.\n\n
   420→11. Measure Performance with Instagram Insights:\n
   421→Regularly review Instagram Insights to understand your audience demographics, post reach, and engagement. Use these insights to refine your strategies.\n\n
   422→12. Utilize Instagram Ads:\n
   423→Invest in Instagram Ads to reach a broader audience and promote your products or services effectively.\n\n
   424→13. Monitor Your Competitors:\n
   425→Keep an eye on your competitors' Instagram activities to gain insights into what works and what doesn't in your industry.\n\n
   426→14. Leverage Instagram Shopping Features:\n
   427→Use Instagram Shopping to tag products in your posts and Stories, making it easy for users to shop directly from your content.\n\n
   428→15. Showcase Customer Testimonials:\n
   429→Feature positive customer reviews and testimonials to build trust and credibility.\n\n
   430→16. Analyze and Adjust Your Strategy:\n
   431→Regularly analyze the performance of your Instagram efforts and adjust your strategy based on the results.\n\n
   432→By following these tips and best practices, you'll be well on your way to establishing a strong presence on Instagram and driving business growth. Remember, consistency and authenticity are key to success on this vibrant social platform.`

## Key Component Patterns

1. **State Management:** Props-driven for loggedIn status passed from parent/App level
2. **Routing:** React Router DOM (Link, useNavigate components)
3. **Forms:** Controlled inputs with useState, email validation regex
4. **Data Fetching:** All data from local JS files in /data directory

6. **UI Components:** Headless UI (Disclosure for accordion), SVG icons inline
7. **Responsive Design:** Mobile-first with Tailwind breakpoints (sm, md, lg, xl)

---

## Data Files Summary

### **src/data/team.js**
- **Export Shape:** Array of team member objects
- **Structure:**
  ```javascript
  {
    name: string,
    role: string,
    imageUrl: string,
    twitterUrl: string,
    linkedinUrl: string
  }
  ```
- **Items:** 6 team members

### **src/data/features.js**
- **Export Shape:** Array of feature objects
- **Structure:**
  ```javascript
  {
    name: string,
    description: string,
    icon: image/PNG
  }
  ```
- **Items:** 6 features with icons imported from assets

### **src/data/blogPostsData.js**
- **Export Shape:** Array of blog post objects
- **Structure:**
  ```javascript
  {
    title: string,
    href: string,
    description: string,
    date: string,
    datetime: string,
    imageUrl: string,
    readingTime: string,
    author: { name: string, imageUrl: string },
    blogPost: string (full content)
  }
  ```
- **Items:** 10 blog posts

### **src/data/faq.js**
- **Export Shape:** Array of FAQ objects
- **Structure:**
  ```javascript
  {
    question: string,
    answer: string
  }
  ```
- **Items:** 8 FAQ entries

### **src/data/pricingTiers.js**
- **Export Shape:** Exported constant array `pricingTiers`
- **Structure:**
  ```javascript
  {
    name: string,
    href: string,
    priceMonthly: number,
    description: string
  }
  ```
- **Items:** 3 tiers (Standard, Premium, Business)

### **src/data/partners.js**
- **Export Shape:** Array of partner objects
- **Structure:**
  ```javascript
  {
    id: number,
    name: string,
    img: string (URL),
    href: string (URL)
  }
  ```
- **Items:** 5 partners (Airbnb, Duolingo, Final Cut, Fiverr, Figma)

### **src/data/companyStats.js**
- **Export Shape:** Array of stat objects
- **Structure:**
  ```javascript
  {
    id: number,
    stat: string,
    title: string,
    desc: string
  }
  ```
- **Items:** 4 stats (Happy Customers, Followers Gained, Engagement, Leads)

### **src/data/pricingSections.js**
- **Export Shape:** Exported constant array `pricingSections`
- **Structure:**
  ```javascript
  {
    name: string (Features/Reporting/Support),
    features: [
      {
        name: string,
        tiers: { Standard?: boolean|string, Premium?: boolean|string, Business?: boolean|string }
      }
    ]
  }
  ```
- **Items:** 3 sections with 9 features (Features), 4 (Reporting), 6 (Support)

### **src/data/companyInformation.js**
- **Export Shape:** Array of info objects
- **Structure:**
  ```javascript
  {
    label: string,
    value: string
  }
  ```
- **Items:** 4 information entries (Founded, Employees, Beta Users, Raised)

### **src/data/jobs.js**
- **Export Shape:** Array of job objects
- **Structure:**
  ```javascript
  {
    department: string,
    role: string,
    imageUrl: string,
    desc: string
  }
  ```
- **Items:** 4 job listings

### **src/data/pricingFaq.js**
- **Export Shape:** Array of FAQ objects
- **Structure:**
  ```javascript
  {
    id: number,
    question: string,
    answer: string
  }
  ```
- **Items:** 6 pricing-related FAQ entries

---

## Key Component Patterns

1. **State Management:** Props-driven for loggedIn status passed from parent/App level
2. **Routing:** React Router DOM (Link, useNavigate components)
3. **Forms:** Controlled inputs with useState, email validation regex
4. **Data Fetching:** All data from local JS files in /data directory
5. **Styling:** Tailwind CSS with custom CSS variables for theming
6. **UI Components:** Headless UI (Disclosure for accordion), SVG icons inline
7. **Responsive Design:** Mobile-first with Tailwind breakpoints (sm, md, lg, xl)

Copy real patterns from the codebase │ - Keep the app code unchanged │ - Put the default setup in `.storybook/preview.tsx` │ - Keep app mocking and runtime setup in `.storybook/preview.tsx`, not │ in the stories │ ### Storybook Documentation Reference │ Use the following references to look up Storybook APIs, concepts, or │ examples: │ - which providers exist │ - which CSS files are injected │ - which queries fetch data │ - which browser-state reads happen │ - which portals and portal roots exist │ - which pages and components show the real usage patterns │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // src/main.tsx │ import "./index.css"; │ import App from "./App"; │ import { SessionProvider } from "./contexts/SessionContext"; │ createRoot(document.getElementById("root")!).render( │ │ │ , │ ); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should copy: │ - the `index.css` import │ - the `SessionProvider` │ - the same provider order │ Example of tracing the app deeper: │ ```tsx │ // src/App.tsx │ function App() { │ const { products, loadMoreProducts } = useProducts(); │ const { currentUser, signOut } = useSession(); │ // ... │ } │ ``` │ ```ts │ // src/hooks/useProducts.ts │ const response = await fetch(apiBaseUrl + "/products?page=1"); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // src/hooks/useTheme.ts │ const savedTheme = localStorage.getItem("theme"); │ ``` │ That means the default Storybook setup should discover and prepare: │ - provider state │ - MSW handlers for queries │ - browser-state values that are actually read during render │ ### Step 2: Build one default app environment in preview │ Set up Storybook once so most stories work without story-specific │ setup. │ Start with the smallest faithful environment: │ - the real provider tree │ - the real root CSS │ - seeded browser state if the app reads it during render │ - MSW for network/data queries │ It is fine to seed browser state such as `localStorage`, │ `sessionStorage`, and cookies when the app reads them during render. │ Seed only the specific app-owned keys and values you need. │ Do not clear all `localStorage`, `sessionStorage`, or cookies, and do │ not reset Storybook's own state. │ Do not mock or redefine the browser runtime itself. │ The stories run in Vitest browser mode, so the real browser │ environment should already exist. │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; │ import { SessionProvider } from '../src/contexts/SessionContext'; │ export default definePreview({ │ decorators: [ │ (Story) => ( │ │ │ │ ), │ ], │ async beforeEach() { │ localStorage.setItem('theme', 'dark'); │ localStorage.setItem('sidebar:open', 'true'); │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ Use this same idea for: │ - providers │ - root CSS │ - browser state │ - dates, and if the app logic depends on them during render then │ always use `mockdate` │ Example with the `mockdate` package: │ ```tsx │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; │ export default definePreview({ │ async beforeEach() { │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ ### Step 3: Support portals with preview-body.html │ If the app uses portals, copy that setup into Storybook too. │ Look for patterns like: │ - `createPortal(...)` │ - modal, dialog, drawer, popover, tooltip, toast, or dropdown portal │ components │ - hard-coded roots such as `#portal-root`, `#modal-root`, │ `#drawer-root`, or `#toast-root` │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // real component │ return createPortal(, │ document.getElementById("portal-root")!); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should create the same portal root in │ `.storybook/preview-body.html`: │ ```html │ │
│ ``` │ If the app uses multiple portal roots, create all of them there: │ ```html │ │
│
│
│ ``` │ If a library portals directly to `document.body`, do not add extra │ roots for it. │ Make sure the copied page shell, CSS, and layout still allow overlays, │ fixed positioning, and z-index stacking to render correctly. │ ### Step 4: Mock side effects globally │ All network/data queries should be handled by the default Storybook │ environment. │ - Always use `msw-storybook-addon` for query mocking. │ - If you introduce MSW, run `npx msw init ./public --save` to create │ the worker file. │ - Make sure Storybook serves `./public` as a static dir so │ `mockServiceWorker.js` is available. │ - Do not mock `fetch` directly. │ - Network/data queries should return deterministic mock data. │ - If you need to change dependencies, first check the lockfile and use │ that package manager for the change. │ Example of copying a real fetch pattern into shared handlers: │ ```ts │ // real app hook │ const response = await fetch( │ apiBaseUrl + │ "/products?" + │ new URLSearchParams({ │ page: "1", │ sort: "featured", │ }), │ ); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/msw-handlers.ts │ import { http, HttpResponse } from "msw"; │ export const mswHandlers = { │ products: [ │ http.get("]8;;https://api.example.com/products",https://api.example.com/products",]8;; │ () => │ HttpResponse.json({ │ items: [ │ { │ id: "product-1", │ name: "Example product", │ description: "Mock product description", │ imageUrl: │ "]8;;https://images.example.com/product.jpg",https://images.example.com/product.jpg",]8;; │ price: 42, │ }, │ ], │ }), │ ), │ ], │ }; │ ``` │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; │ import { initialize, mswLoader } from 'msw-storybook-addon'; │ import { mswHandlers } from './msw-handlers'; │ initialize({ │ onUnhandledRequest: 'bypass', │ }); │ export default definePreview({ │ loaders: [mswLoader], │ parameters: { │ msw: { │ handlers: mswHandlers, │ }, │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/main.ts │ import type { StorybookConfig } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ const config: StorybookConfig = { │ staticDirs: ["../public"], │ }; │ export default config; │ ``` │ Keep these mocks global. │ Do not put fetch mocks in individual stories. │ Only add handlers for requests that the shared preview setup or the │ stories actually use. │ Do not add catch-all handlers that can hide unrelated failures. │ If the defaults are not enough, improve the shared default setup │ instead. │ Seed browser state when needed, but do not mock `window`, `document`, │ `navigator`, observers, or similar runtime APIs. │ The only exception is `mockdate` when date-based rendering exists. │ ### Step 5: Write stories │ Try to find around 10 good candidate components for story files. │ Write colocated stories for top-level components, from low-level │ reusable components up to page components. │ Write up to 10 story files, or fewer only if the codebase clearly has │ fewer meaningful targets. │ The stories should use JSX copied from real usage patterns in: │ - pages │ - app shells │ - routes │ - tests │ - existing feature code │ As a rule of thumb, each story file should have around 3 story exports │ when the component or page has enough meaningful states. │ It can have more when the real usage supports it, up to 10 story │ exports in one file. │ Always show all imports explicitly in story and preview files. │ Do not rely on omitted or implied imports in examples or generated │ code. │ #### Story tags │ Every story meta must include the `ai-generated` tag to identify │ AI-created stories: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { SomeComponent } from './SomeComponent'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const Default = meta.story({ │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ If a story could not be fully fixed after the self-healing loop (the │ test still fails │ or the rendering is incomplete), add the `needs-work` tag alongside │ `ai-generated`: │ ```ts │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated', 'needs-work'], │ }); │ ``` │ #### Args vs render │ For simple components where props drive the state, prefer `args` │ stories — no `render` function needed: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { Button } from './Button'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: Button, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const Primary = meta.story({ │ args: { │ variant: 'primary', │ children: 'Save', │ }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i │ })).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }); │ export const Disabled = meta.story({ │ args: { │ variant: 'primary', │ disabled: true, │ children: 'Save', │ }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeDisabled(); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ Use `render` when the story needs composition — wrapping the component │ in layout, combining multiple components, or passing children as JSX: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { Button } from './Button'; │ import { Card } from './Card'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: Button, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const InsideCard = meta.story({ │ render: () => ( │ │ │ │ ), │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i │ })).toBeVisible(); │ await userEvent.click(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i })); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ Keep app mocking and runtime setup in preview, not in the stories. │ Do not build large story-specific harnesses. │ Do not write story files for subcomponents, hooks, contexts, or │ helpers. │ Do not create new application components. │ Do not add a custom `title`. │ Do not stop after only a few easy targets if the codebase has more │ meaningful components or pages available. │ ### Step 6: Write a play function for every story │ Every named story export must have a `play` function. │ The `play` function is not optional, even for simple stories. │ The purpose of the `play` function is to prove that the story actually │ works in the copied Storybook environment: │ - the story renders something real and non-empty │ - the decorators provide the needed context │ - the CSS is applied well enough for the intended state to be visible │ - the MSW mocks or seeded browser state are actually being used │ - important interactions, async loading states, and portals behave │ correctly │ Use `play` functions to verify behavior, not just to click around. │ A story without assertions is incomplete. │ Use tools from `storybook/test` such as: │ - `expect` │ - `waitFor` │ Prefer `canvas` and `userEvent` from the `play` context. │ Do not destructure `canvasElement` just to create `const canvas = │ within(canvasElement)`. │ Do not import `userEvent` from `storybook/test`; use `userEvent` from │ the `play` context instead. │ Only use `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when you need to query outside │ the canvas, such as for portals. │ Example: │ ```tsx │ import type { StoryObj } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ export const FilledForm: Story = { │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ const emailInput = canvas.getByLabelText("email", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(emailInput, "example-email@email.com", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const passwordInput = canvas.getByLabelText("password", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(passwordInput, "ExamplePassword", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const submitButton = canvas.getByRole("button"); │ await userEvent.click(submitButton); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ The assertions should match the real pattern you copied: │ - for provider-backed stories, assert the provider-dependent UI │ appears correctly │ - for mocked-data stories, wait for the mocked data to appear and │ assert on it │ - for CSS-sensitive states, assert on visibility, text layout, │ class-driven states, or meaningful computed styles │ - for routing or navigation stories, assert the routed state or │ navigation outcome │ - for portal stories, query from `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when │ the UI renders outside the canvas │ Examples of useful checks: │ - a themed button has the expected label and is visibly enabled or │ disabled │ - a modal opened through a decorator or provider is visible in the │ portal root │ - mocked API data appears in the page instead of a loading spinner │ forever │ - a selected tab actually shows the selected panel │ - a toast, alert, or badge has the expected accessible text and visual │ state │ - a CSS class or computed style confirms the real state that matters │ ### Step 7: Prove CSS is loaded in exactly one story named `CssCheck` │ In exactly one story, named `CssCheck`, assert a component-specific │ computed style. `toBeVisible` passes on an unstyled component; a │ concrete style value proves the shared preview loaded the app's CSS. │ Pick a visually distinctive component, read a styling value from its │ source, and assert it with `getComputedStyle`: │ ```tsx │ export const CssCheck: Story = { │ args: { children: "Submit" }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ const button = canvas.getByRole("button", { name: /submit/i }); │ // PrimaryButton uses bg-blue-600 — fails if Tailwind / global CSS did │ not load. │ await expect(getComputedStyle(button).backgroundColor).toBe("rgb(37, │ 99, 235)"); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ ### Step 8: Cover the patterns you found │ Write stories for the real patterns in the codebase, for example: │ - a low-level reusable component in real JSX usage │ - a provider-backed component │ - a browser-state-backed component │ - a fetched-data component │ - a real page component │ Use `App.tsx` to inspect the real provider tree and usage patterns, │ but do not make a story for `App` when the codebase has actual page │ components. │ Example page story: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { ProductPage } from './ProductPage'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: ProductPage, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const Default = meta.story({ │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect( │ canvas.getByRole('heading', { name: /products/i }), │ ).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ ### Step 9: Verify both rendering and types │ As you work, verify the stories with Vitest: │ ```bash │ npx vitest --project storybook │ ``` │ Also verify types so you catch missing required props, broken imports, │ and preview typing issues. Run the same TypeScript command the project │ itself uses. │ ```bash │ │ ``` │ After verification passes, review every changed file and remove │ anything that is not needed for the final solution, especially debug │ fixes, overly broad mocks, unnecessary dependencies, and eval │ artifacts. │ Keep iterating until: │ - every story you wrote passes │ - every story you wrote has a meaningful passing `play` function │ - the changed stories and preview setup pass the project's real │ TypeScript check │ - the rendered output looks sensible │ - the default global mocked environment is strong enough that stories │ do not need manual fetch overrides │ - stories no longer fail because the shared preview setup and story │ JSX are fixed │ - all passing stories have `tags: ['ai-generated']` in their meta │ - any stories that still need work have `tags: ['ai-generated', │ 'needs-work']` in their meta

![Edgy](https://github.com/catherineisonline/edgy/blob/main/public/project-preview.png?raw=true) # Edgy - #1 Instagram Growth Agency [![GitHub license](https://img.shields.io/github/license/catherineisonline/edgy)](https://github.com/catherineisonline/edgy/blob/main/LICENSE) [![GitHub issues](https://img.shields.io/github/issues/catherineisonline/edgy)](https://github.com/catherineisonline/edgy/issues) [![GitHub stars](https://img.shields.io/github/stars/catherineisonline/edgy)](https://github.com/catherineisonline/edgy/stargazers) ## Introduction Welcome to the Edgy Social Media Marketing Agency's full-stack website! At Edgy, we're not just a business; we're your partners in navigating the dynamic landscape of Instagram marketing. Whether you're a budding influencer or a business looking to boost your brand's online visibility, we're here to help you stand out in the digital crowd. #### About Edgy: Edgy is a fictional social media marketing agency that specializes in Instagram growth strategies. Our team of passionate experts understands the ever-changing algorithms, trends, and techniques that define success on Instagram. With our tailored services and creative solutions, we're dedicated to helping you harness the power of this platform to its fullest potential. #### What We Offer: Comprehensive Services: Our website offers a diverse range of services designed to cater to both businesses and beginner influencers. From content creation and engagement strategies to influencer collaborations and brand partnerships, we've got you covered. Flexible Pricing Options: We understand that every journey is unique, which is why we offer a variety of pricing plans that can be customized to suit your specific needs and budget. Whether you're just starting out or looking to scale, we have a plan for you. Engaging Blog Content: Stay up-to-date with the latest trends, tips, and insights in the world of Instagram marketing through our informative blog. Our team of experts shares valuable information to empower you on your digital journey. Career Opportunities: Passionate about social media marketing? Explore exciting career prospects with Edgy on our dedicated career page. Join our team of creatives and strategists to make a lasting impact in the digital marketing space. #### Get in Touch: Contact Form: Have questions, inquiries, or simply want to say hello? Our contact form is designed to make reaching out to us quick and easy. We value your input and are always ready to assist you. ## Disclaimer Please note that Edgy is a fictional business created for illustrative purposes. While our website and services are designed to provide insights into the world of social media marketing, we do not provide real services or consultations. This platform is intended to showcase a hypothetical business model. Thank you for visiting Edgy's website. We look forward to being a part of your journey towards Instagram success! Disclaimer: This is a fictional business created for demonstration purposes. ## Features The website offers a seamless user experience through its comprehensive account management features. Users have the ability to perform essential actions such as signing up, logging in, and modifying their profile information. #### Sign Up and Login: - Users can easily create an account by providing their email address and password during the registration process. - Upon successful registration, users are granted access to their personalized accounts, enabling them to log in securely. #### Profile Information: - The user's profile is designed to be minimalistic, containing essential details to maintain simplicity and user-friendliness.
